title: Gambler's Ruin

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A gambler starts with $i. Each game, they bet $1: win with probability p, lose with probability q = 1 - p. They stop when they reach $0 (ruin) or $N (target).
rules: Game ends when wealth reaches 0 or N.
setup.question: What is P_i = P(gambler reaches N starting from $i)?

learningObjectives: Set up the recurrence P_i = p * P_{i+1} + q * P_{i-1}.
Solve the recurrence to get the closed form (case p != 1/2 vs p = 1/2).

stages.label: Set up the recurrence
stages.prompt: Let P_i = P(reach N | start at i). Condition on the next bet's outcome.

options.text: P_i = p * P_{i+1} + q * P_{i-1}, with P_0 = 0 and P_N = 1.

stages.explanation: First-step analysis: condition on win (prob p, move to i+1) or lose (prob q, move to i-1). Boundaries: P_0 = 0 (ruined, can't recover), P_N = 1 (target reached).

stages.label: Convert to a difference equation
stages.prompt: Let d_i = P_i - P_{i-1}. From P_i = p P_{i+1} + q P_{i-1}, derive a recurrence for d_i.

options.text: d_{i+1} = (q/p) * d_i.  Setting r = q/p, the differences form a geometric sequence with ratio r.

stages.explanation: Rearrange: p P_{i+1} - P_i = -q P_{i-1}, so p (P_{i+1} - P_i) = q (P_i - P_{i-1}) - 0 (need to redo). Actually: P_i - P_{i-1} = p (P_{i+1} - P_i) when set up via 'rate of change'. Cleanly: from p P_{i+1} - P_i + q P_{i-1} = 0, with p + q = 1, get p (P_{i+1} - P_i) = q (P_i - P_{i-1}), so d_{i+1} = (q/p) d_i.

stages.label: Solve the geometric sum
stages.prompt: Sum d_1 + d_2 + ... + d_N = P_N - P_0 = 1. With d_i = d_1 * r^(i-1) where r = q/p:
stages.explanation: Geometric sum: when r != 1, sum d_1 * r^(i-1) for i=1..N = d_1 * (1 - r^N)/(1 - r) = 1, so d_1 = (1 - r)/(1 - r^N). Then P_i = sum d_j for j=1..i = d_1 * (1 - r^i)/(1 - r) = (1 - r^i)/(1 - r^N). When r = 1 (fair game), all d_i are equal -- so P_i = i/N.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: Two cases. Fair game: linear in i. Unfair game: geometric in i. For the unfair case with p < 1/2 (gambler loses more often than wins), P_i decays exponentially -- ruin is overwhelmingly likely if N >> i.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: If p = 0.49 (slight house edge), starting at $50 with target $100, what's P(reach 100)? Verify it's near 0.
What's P(ruin) if N -> infinity (no upper bound, just stop at 0)?
Does the EXPECTED ending wealth matter? Hint: consider the limit p -> 1/2.
